result.markdown:
# Logistic Regression Example: Proposal {#sec-logistic-proposal} ::: {.callout-note} ## About This Chapter This chapter demonstrates **Parts 1--3** of the logistic regression project using the PIAAC Korea dataset. The same data and predictor variables from Project 1 are reused, but the outcome changes to a **binary** variable: whether a worker holds a supervisory (managerial) position. Each section begins with a guideline callout, followed by the example text. ::: ## Part 1: Background and Research Question {#sec-logistic-ex-part1} ::: {.callout-important} ## Guideline Reminder --- Part 1 Provide a brief paragraph motivating **why** the topic matters, then state **one focused research question** involving a binary outcome. Name the modeled event explicitly: which outcome category is $Y = 1$? Do **not** describe the data or variables here --- save that for Part 2. (See @sec-logistic-part1 for the full expectations.) ::: ### Background Beyond earnings, career advancement to supervisory positions is another important labor market outcome. Supervisory roles are often associated with higher wages, greater autonomy, and greater influence over organizational decisions. Understanding which individual characteristics are associated with supervisory status can help us think more carefully about labor-market advancement and inequality. In South Korea, where educational attainment is nearly universal at the tertiary level, the traditional credential signal may have limited power to differentiate workers for promotion into supervisory roles. Cognitive skills measured directly --- such as numeracy --- may provide additional predictive value beyond years of schooling. Furthermore, a persistent gender gap in leadership positions raises the question of whether education and skill associations with supervisory status differ between men and women. This project examines how human capital, cognitive skills, occupation, and gender relate to supervisory status within the supplied PIAAC Korea teaching subset. ### Research Question **Research question:** Among Korean full-time workers, how are gender, education, work experience, occupational classification, and numeracy associated with the probability of holding a supervisory position (`manager = 1`), including whether the education or numeracy associations differ by gender? ::: {.callout-tip} ## Writing Tip Project 2 uses **one focused logistic research question**. The planned models below still build in stages --- baseline model, extended model, interaction model --- but those stages support the same overarching question rather than creating separate numbered research questions. ::: ## Part 2: Methods --- Subjects and Variables {#sec-logistic-ex-part2} ::: {.callout-important} ## Guideline Reminder --- Part 2 Describe the **data source** and study design, the **subjects** and final analytic sample size, the **missing-data rule**, and provide a table listing **every variable** with its name, type, coding, and definition. For the binary outcome, identify the event and non-event. For categorical predictors, list all categories and the reference group. (See @sec-logistic-part2 for the full expectations.) ::: ### Study Design and Data Source We use the same data from the **Programme for the International Assessment of Adult Competencies** [PIAAC; @oecd2013piaac] described in @sec-linear-ex-part2. The PIAAC provides detailed information on adult skills and labor market outcomes, including supervisory responsibilities. ### Subjects The supplied analysis file is the same already-filtered Korean PIAAC subset used in Project 1. It contains prime working-age adults (35--54) who were full-time employees. After applying the complete-case rule to the planned logistic outcome and predictors, the final analytic sample includes **$N$ = 1,357** respondents in the teaching file. As in Project 1, this is an unweighted teaching subset. The logistic models do not use PIAAC survey weights, replicate weights, plausible values, or complex sample design information, so inference is limited to this teaching analysis file. Interpret odds ratios and predicted probabilities as sample associations with supervisory status in this subset, not as nationally representative estimates. ### Variables Because this project reuses the same predictor variables from Project 1, we focus on describing the **new outcome variable** and provide a complete variable table for reference. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} analysis_vars <- c( "manager", "female", "schyrs_c", "exper_c", "exper2_c", "occupation", "numeracy" ) analysis_df <- readRDS(here::here("data", "piaac_kor.rds")) |> mutate(occupation = fct_relevel(occupation, "Skilled")) |> drop_na(all_of(analysis_vars)) tibble( final_n = nrow(analysis_df), modeled_event = sum(analysis_df$manager == 1), non_event = sum(analysis_df$manager == 0), occupation_reference = levels(analysis_df$occupation)[1] ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| final_n| modeled_event| non_event|occupation_reference | |-------:|-------------:|---------:|:--------------------| | 1357| 580| 777|Skilled |
::: ::: ::: {.callout-note} As in the linear regression example (@sec-linear-proposal), we set **Skilled** as the reference category for occupation. See Chapter 4 for the rationale. ::: **Outcome variable.** The outcome is `manager`, a binary indicator of supervisory status. The modeled event is `manager = 1`: the respondent supervises other employees as part of their job. The non-event is `manager = 0`: the respondent does not supervise other employees. This variable is derived from the PIAAC item asking whether the respondent manages or supervises other workers at their current job. @tbl-manager-distribution shows the number and percentage of workers in the modeled event and non-event categories. ::: {#tbl-manager-distribution .cell tbl-cap='Distribution of the outcome variable (manager).'} ```{.r .cell-code} # Frequency table of the modeled event and non-event manager_freq <- table(analysis_df$manager) manager_pct <- round(prop.table(manager_freq) * 100, 1) manager_tbl <- tibble::tibble( `Supervisory Status` = c( "Non-manager (0, non-event)", "Manager (1, event)", "Total" ), n = c(manager_freq[1], manager_freq[2], sum(manager_freq)), `%` = c(manager_pct[1], manager_pct[2], 100.0) ) kable(manager_tbl, align = "lrr") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} |Supervisory Status | n| %| |:--------------------------|----:|-----:| |Non-manager (0, non-event) | 777| 57.3| |Manager (1, event) | 580| 42.7| |Total | 1357| 100.0| ::: ::: The sample contains **580 managers (42.7%)** and **777 non-managers (57.3%)**. The outcome is not extremely rare, so logistic regression is an appropriate modeling choice with no need for specialized rare-event methods. **Predictor variables.** All predictor variables are identical to those used in Project 1. @tbl-logistic-variables provides the complete variable table. ::: {#tbl-logistic-variables .cell tbl-cap='Variables used in the logistic regression analysis of supervisory status in the PIAAC Korea teaching subset.'} ```{.r .cell-code} var_table <- tibble::tribble( ~Variable, ~Role, ~Type, ~Definition, "`manager`", "Outcome", "Binary", "1 = supervises other employees (event), 0 = does not (non-event)", "`female`", "Predictor", "Binary", "1 = female, 0 = male (ref)", "`schyrs_c`", "Predictor", "Continuous", "Mean-centered years of education", "`exper_c`", "Predictor", "Continuous", "Mean-centered work experience (in decades)", "`exper2_c`", "Predictor", "Continuous", "Squared term of mean-centered experience", "`occupation`", "Predictor", "Categorical (4 levels)", "Skilled (ref), Semi-white, Semi-blue, Elementary", "`numeracy`", "Predictor", "Continuous", "Z-scored numeracy proficiency (1 unit = 1 SD)" ) kable(var_table, align = "llll") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} |Variable |Role |Type |Definition | |:------------|:---------|:----------------------|:----------------------------------------------------------------| |`manager` |Outcome |Binary |1 = supervises other employees (event), 0 = does not (non-event) | |`female` |Predictor |Binary |1 = female, 0 = male (ref) | |`schyrs_c` |Predictor |Continuous |Mean-centered years of education | |`exper_c` |Predictor |Continuous |Mean-centered work experience (in decades) | |`exper2_c` |Predictor |Continuous |Squared term of mean-centered experience | |`occupation` |Predictor |Categorical (4 levels) |Skilled (ref), Semi-white, Semi-blue, Elementary | |`numeracy` |Predictor |Continuous |Z-scored numeracy proficiency (1 unit = 1 SD) | ::: ::: ::: {.callout-tip} ## Writing Tip When the same predictors are used across projects, you do not need to repeat lengthy descriptions of every variable. Instead, reference the earlier project and focus your writing on what has changed --- in this case, the outcome variable and the rationale for using logistic regression. ::: ## Part 3: Methods --- Statistical Analysis {#sec-logistic-ex-part3} ::: {.callout-important} ## Guideline Reminder --- Part 3 Write out your logistic regression model(s) using proper notation. If you use a model comparison strategy (e.g., nested models), describe each model stage and explain how it supports the single research question. State your significance level. (See @sec-logistic-part3 for the full expectations.) ::: ### Analytic Strategy We estimate a planned sequence of **three nested logistic regression models** to build evidence for the single research question. Model 1 estimates a baseline human-capital specification, Model 2 adds cognitive skill and occupation, and Model 3 tests whether the education and numeracy associations differ by gender. All three models use the same complete-case `analysis_df`, so model comparisons are based on the same $N = 1{,}357$ rows. All analyses are conducted in R (version 4.4+). We set the significance level at $\alpha = 0.05$ for all statistical tests. Results will be presented as **odds ratios** with 95% confidence intervals, and we will compute **predicted probabilities** to illustrate practical significance. Let $Y_i = 1$ if worker $i$ supervises other employees and $Y_i = 0$ otherwise. Let $p_i = P(Y_i = 1)$. ### Model 1: Baseline Human Capital Model {#sec-logistic-ex-model1} The first model regresses supervisory status on gender, education, and work experience. The baseline model is shown in @eq-logistic-model1. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{logit}(p_i) ={} & \beta_0 + \beta_1 \, \text{female}_i + \beta_2 \, \text{schyrs\_c}_i + \beta_3 \, \text{exper\_c}_i \\ & + \beta_4 \, \text{exper2\_c}_i \end{aligned} $$ {#eq-logistic-model1} where $\text{logit}(p) = \ln\!\big(\frac{p}{1-p}\big)$. This baseline stage estimates the association of gender, education, and experience with the odds of the modeled event, `manager = 1`. The exponentiated coefficient $e^{\beta_2}$ gives the **odds ratio** for a one-year increase in education, holding all other variables constant. The intercept $\beta_0$ represents the log-odds of being a manager for a male worker with average education and average experience because both predictors are mean-centered. ### Model 2: Adding Cognitive Skills and Occupation {#sec-logistic-ex-model2} The second model extends Model 1 by adding occupational classification and numeracy proficiency. The extended model is shown in @eq-logistic-model2. For compact notation, define $D_{SW,i}$, $D_{SB,i}$, and $D_{EL,i}$ as indicators for the semi-white, semi-blue, and elementary occupation categories, respectively. Skilled workers are the reference group. In R, we use `occupation` as a factor and let R create these contrasts automatically; the course data also include equivalent 0/1 columns (`semiwhite`, `semiblue`, `elementary`) if you want to inspect the coding. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{logit}(p_i) ={} & \beta_0 + \beta_1 \, \text{female}_i + \beta_2 \, \text{schyrs\_c}_i + \beta_3 \, \text{exper\_c}_i + \beta_4 \, \text{exper2\_c}_i \\ & + \beta_5 \, D_{SW,i} + \beta_6 \, D_{SB,i} + \beta_7 \, D_{EL,i} + \beta_8 \, \text{numeracy}_i \end{aligned} $$ {#eq-logistic-model2} This stage tests whether occupation and numeracy add evidence about supervisory status beyond the baseline human-capital variables. We compare Model 1 and Model 2 using a **likelihood ratio test** (the logistic regression analogue of the incremental $F$-test) to determine whether the additional predictors significantly improve model fit. Key coefficients of interest: - $e^{\beta_8}$ (numeracy): the multiplicative change in the odds of being a manager for a one-SD increase in numeracy, holding all other variables constant. - $e^{\beta_5}$, $e^{\beta_6}$, $e^{\beta_7}$: the odds of being a manager in each listed occupation category relative to the skilled reference group. ### Model 3: Gender Interactions {#sec-logistic-ex-model3} The third model extends Model 2 by adding interaction terms between gender and two key predictors --- education and numeracy. The interaction model is shown in @eq-logistic-model3. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{logit}(p_i) ={} & \beta_0 + \beta_1 \, \text{female}_i + \beta_2 \, \text{schyrs\_c}_i + \beta_3 \, \text{exper\_c}_i + \beta_4 \, \text{exper2\_c}_i \\ & + \beta_5 \, D_{SW,i} + \beta_6 \, D_{SB,i} + \beta_7 \, D_{EL,i} + \beta_8 \, \text{numeracy}_i \\ & + \beta_9 \, (\text{female}_i \times \text{schyrs\_c}_i) + \beta_{10} \, (\text{female}_i \times \text{numeracy}_i) \end{aligned} $$ {#eq-logistic-model3} This stage tests whether the education and numeracy associations with the log-odds of the modeled event differ between men and women: - $\beta_9 > 0$ would indicate that the education log-odds association is larger for women than for men. - $\beta_{10} > 0$ would indicate that the numeracy log-odds association is larger for women than for men. Because interaction terms in logistic regression are on the log-odds scale, the probability-scale implications will be interpreted using predicted probabilities in the results chapter. We test the significance of the interaction block using a likelihood ratio test comparing Model 2 and Model 3. ### Summary of Model Stages @tbl-logistic-model-summary summarizes how the planned nested model stages support the single logistic research question. | Model | Predictors Added | Purpose for the Single Research Question | |-------|------------------|------------------------------------------| | Model 1 | `female`, `schyrs_c`, `exper_c`, `exper2_c` | Baseline association of gender, education, and experience with the odds of `manager = 1` | | Model 2 | + `occupation` factor contrasts, `numeracy` | Tests whether cognitive skill and occupation add evidence about supervisory status | | Model 3 | + `female` $\times$ `schyrs_c`, `female` $\times$ `numeracy` | Tests whether education and numeracy associations differ by gender | : Summary of the planned logistic model stages. {#tbl-logistic-model-summary} ### Model Evaluation For logistic regression, we will assess model quality using the following: 1. **Overall model fit:** Model $N$ and log-likelihood from `nobs()` and `logLik()`, plus AIC, BIC, Tjur's $R^2$, and RMSE from `model_fit()`. 2. **Model comparison:** Likelihood ratio tests comparing the planned nested model stages (analogous to the incremental $F$-test in linear regression). 3. **Sparse cells and separation:** Outcome-by-category tables, convergence warnings, unusually large standard errors, and fitted probabilities close to 0 or 1. 4. **Categorical predictor block:** An overall likelihood ratio test for the multi-category occupation predictor. 5. **Linearity in the logit:** A simple check for the continuous predictors before relying on a linear logit specification. 6. **Multicollinearity:** Variance inflation factors for the full logistic model, using `performance::check_collinearity()`; values above 5 will be flagged for discussion. 7. **Influence:** Cook's distance or related influence screening, followed by a sensitivity check if influential observations appear to drive conclusions. ::: {.callout-tip} ## Writing Tip Logistic regression does not have the same residual-based assumption checks as linear regression (no normality or homoscedasticity of residuals). Instead, focus on overall model fit statistics, the likelihood ratio test, and predicted probabilities. Mentioning your evaluation plan in the proposal shows methodological awareness. ::: ### R Setup for the Analysis The code below shows the R commands we will use to fit the three models. The results will be reported and interpreted in @sec-logistic-results. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # --- Model 1: Baseline human capital model --- logit1 <- glm(manager ~ female + schyrs_c + exper_c + exper2_c, data = analysis_df, family = binomial) # --- Model 2: Add occupation and numeracy --- logit2 <- glm(manager ~ female + schyrs_c + exper_c + exper2_c + occupation + numeracy, data = analysis_df, family = binomial) # --- Model 3: Add gender interactions --- logit3 <- glm(manager ~ female + schyrs_c + exper_c + exper2_c + occupation + numeracy + female:schyrs_c + female:numeracy, data = analysis_df, family = binomial) # --- Odds ratios, fit, and planned model comparisons --- model_params(logit3, exponentiate = TRUE) model_fit(logit3) logLik(logit3) performance::check_collinearity(logit3) anova(logit1, logit2, test = "Chisq") # Occupation + numeracy block anova(logit2, logit3, test = "Chisq") # Gender interaction block ``` ::: ::: {.callout-note} ## What Comes Next In @sec-logistic-results, we will fit these three models, report the odds ratios in a combined table, compute predicted probabilities for key comparisons, interpret the results in the context of the single research question, and evaluate model fit. :::
